video_title: How the most AI-pilled product team builds products | Fiona Fung (Claude Code and Cowork)
gist: Fiona Fung, who leads the Claude Code and Cowork teams at Anthropic, describes how an 'AI-pilled' product org operates in 2026: engineers ship ~8x more code than in 2025, coding is no longer the bottleneck, and the constraint has shifted to verification, quality, and staying on top of throughput. She details concrete management techniques (a personal Claude Code remote session across all repos, automated 'routines', JIT planning) and where the hiring profile, role boundaries, and team culture are heading.

themes.theme: Coding throughput and the verification bottleneck

insights.claim: Anthropic engineers on average ship roughly 8x as much code per quarter in early 2026 as they did across 2021-2025, after a long flat period that inflected sharply upward.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A concrete, company-reported productivity multiple from a frontier lab is a benchmark for how far AI-assisted engineering output can shift, and signals where tooling spend is headed.
insights.quote: Anthropic engineers on average ship eight times as much code per quarter as they did compared to 2025.

insights.claim: Once coding stops being the bottleneck, the binding constraint in software development shifts to verification, especially as designers, PMs, and non-engineers all start checking in code.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating dev-tools should weight verification, testing, and quality-assurance tooling as the next bottleneck and value-capture layer, not raw code generation.
insights.quote: coding is no longer the bottleneck. And so now like... where has that shift happened? Like now not only engineers but we also have designers, PMs, everybody on the Clockwork team checks in code.

insights.claim: On Fiona's team most commits are now Claude-assisted, marking a baseline shift in what 'normal' engineering work looks like versus a couple of years ago.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: AI-assisted commits becoming the default, not the exception, validates durable demand for coding-agent products embedded in the core workflow.
insights.quote: definitely I think most commits are cloud assisted and so that was a shift.

insights.claim: Claude Code Reviews did not exist a year earlier, and human reviewers were previously one of the biggest bottlenecks; automated review against checked-in 'specs' now relieves much of that load.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Automated code review is a rapidly emerging product surface; the speed of its adoption inside Anthropic shows how fast review workflows can be displaced.
insights.quote: it's crazy when you think we didn't even have Claude Code Reviews last year. And so speaking of bottlenecks, that that was a really really big bottleneck of, you know, the human reviewers.

themes.theme: Management techniques for an AI-pilled team

insights.claim: Fiona runs a persistent Claude Code remote session enlisted across all team repos with access to Slack channels and metrics, using it for management visibility and monthly retrospectives rather than only generating PRs.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A reusable pattern for managers drowning in 8x throughput; signals demand for agent tooling that aggregates work, feedback, and metrics across an org, not just per-repo coding.
insights.quote: I actually have a cloud code remote session that I enlist in all of our repos. And so, this way I have full visibility into the work that everybody's doing.

insights.claim: Anthropic launched 'routines' (a month or two before the interview) that run on a schedule, spawn agents on the user's behalf, and return ready-to-review PRs, raising the level of abstraction from synchronous prompting to scheduled multi-agent work.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Scheduled, agent-spawning automations are a new product category that converts manager rituals into async pipelines; watch for monetization of 'always-on' agent workflows.
insights.quote: a month or two ago we we launched routines. And that's also completely changed. Like now I just have a routine that automates all this for me

insights.claim: Engineering work is shifting from synchronous prompting toward asynchronous fleets of agents, with routines pulling the abstraction level up so a manager writes a routine that itself generates prompts and spawns agents.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: The 'fleets of agents' / async paradigm is the stated next frontier; tooling that orchestrates and supervises parallel agents is where the next product surface and spend will concentrate.
insights.quote: we're shifting more towards async, like asynchronous. And so to your point, the the fleets of agent, like that that's why routines is so interesting

insights.claim: Fiona endorses a 'make new mistakes' principle: zero mistakes likely means the team is moving too slowly or being too cautious, so the goal is to avoid repeating mistakes rather than to eliminate them.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A risk-tolerance posture that pairs with high velocity; relevant for assessing how fast-moving AI orgs trade off speed against error rates.
insights.quote: Make new mistakes. Like it's okay to make mistakes, just make new ones so that we're always learning cuz if you aim to make zero mistakes, like that probably means you're not... moving fast enough

insights.claim: Anthropic teams are given 'explicit permission to kill processes that no longer serve us', and Fiona advises regularly auditing any dreaded, noisy, or highly manual process for whether it still has a purpose or can be automated.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A cultural mechanism for keeping a fast-changing org lean; useful lens for evaluating operational discipline at high-growth AI companies.
insights.quote: one other thing that's really big on Cloud Code and Core team culture is explicit permission to kill processes that no longer serve us.

insights.claim: Fiona now hires for two profiles: creative builders with product sense (the 'dreamers' who own a product end-to-end) and deep systems/distributed-systems experts for the hard parts that still need verification.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Signals where engineering talent demand bifurcates in an AI-native org: product-minded generalists plus scarce deep specialists, hollowing out the middle.
insights.quote: the two profiles that you now look for when you're hiring are creative builders with product sense and deep systems experts for the hard parts.

insights.claim: Coding-adjacent roles are all shifting: PMs are no longer bottlenecked on engineering bandwidth and now roll up their sleeves to ship features, while engineers must build a stronger product-sense muscle.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Role boundaries are dissolving in both directions; org-design and headcount assumptions (PM:eng ratios, cross-functional dependencies) need rethinking for AI-native teams.
insights.quote: PM is also transformed quite a bit because PM bar no longer bottlenecked if if they have an idea waiting on engineering bandwidth.

insights.claim: Design and data science are the next workflows Fiona expects AI to automate after coding, with data scientists increasingly spending their time reviewing AI-generated analyses that are often wrong.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Identifies the next adjacent markets for AI productivity tools beyond code; data-science and design tooling are candidate expansion areas.
insights.quote: when you think about like design or data science like those are starting to be the next areas I think are good opportunities for us

insights.claim: AI lifts the ceiling of what any individual can build, letting a non-mobile engineer ship mobile surfaces by treating Claude as a partner, which expands the effective scope of each engineer.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: If individuals can credibly span specialties, the case for large per-platform orgs weakens, with downstream implications for engineering headcount structure.
insights.quote: thanks to Claude, actually I can actually have a partner and actually also do this on the mobile surfaces... it's lifted the ceiling of what and anyone is able to do.

insights.claim: Anthropic still needs iOS and Android experts but is moving toward smaller per-platform orgs because people now flex across platforms rather than staffing large dedicated mobile orgs.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A concrete example of AI shrinking specialized org footprints; relevant for forecasting engineering headcount efficiency at AI-native firms.
insights.quote: we definitely feel that it's really important like it's really important to still bring on folks with those expertise, but we probably don't need like as large because people are flexing.

insights.claim: Despite AI raising per-engineer output, Anthropic and OpenAI are hiring engineers aggressively, suggesting AI is increasing rather than reducing aggregate engineering demand for now.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Counters the thesis that coding agents shrink engineering headcount at the frontier; demand for engineers remains strong even as productivity multiplies.
insights.quote: it feels like you guys are hiring engineers like crazy. Open eyes hiring engineers like crazy. Just like there's so much demand for engineers.

insights.claim: Fiona argues no single throughput metric works for AI-era productivity: lines of code, significant lines, and even time-to-land-PR all break down, so she focuses on whether output maps to the intended outcome rather than on motion.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Warns investors and operators against using token spend or LOC as ROI proxies for AI tooling; outcome-linked measurement is the harder, more reliable signal.
insights.quote: don't forsake motion for progress. Um because if you're measuring like... tool user usage, then you're you're measuring the action, but is it really making whatever the end outcome

insights.claim: There is a 'vibe shift' from token-maxing (spend as much as possible to see what's achievable) toward scrutinizing actual ROI and cost of AI usage.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A demand-side shift from experimentation to ROI accountability could pressure AI tooling vendors to justify cost, affecting usage-based revenue durability.
insights.quote: it feels like there's this vibe shift recently from token maxing just go crazy spend as much as possible just see what's possible to like wait what are we actually getting out of this? How much is it costing? What's the ROI?

insights.claim: Boris Cherny, who previously led product/engineering productivity measurement at Meta, is now on the Claude Code team, underscoring that productivity measurement expertise is central to building coding-agent products.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Talent concentration around productivity measurement signals it is a strategic, unsolved problem inside the leading coding-agent team.
insights.quote: Boris was actually like head of product inch productivity at meta that was like his job is measuring inch productivity

insights.claim: For quality, Fiona's team uses a 'bad vs sad' framework where 'bad' is an irrecoverable error (e.g., a CLI crash that loses work) and 'sad' is a recoverable pain point (e.g., flickering), with each team given agency to define its own thresholds.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A reusable quality taxonomy for products with many surfaces; helps teams prioritize across heterogeneous dashboards where raw numbers lack context.
insights.quote: bad is like a very bad irrecoverable error. And sad is something that's kind of like a pain point recoverable, but it's interesting when you stack up sads, it could... generally go to bad.

insights.claim: Anthropic shifted toward proactive quality and earlier detection plus monitoring and tests over more human review, because review cannot keep pace with code velocity, effectively closing the loop so agents know what success looks like.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Investment shifts from manual review toward evals, monitoring, and automated test infrastructure as the scalable way to hold quality at high throughput.
insights.quote: the more that we can automate to almost check in the framework for what good looks like. Claude is very good when you give it a framework to validate against those frameworks.

insights.claim: Checking specs into the repo and keeping them in sync with code lets Claude Code Review validate work against an explicit 'what good looks like' standard, an evolution of test-driven development now made cheap because the model writes the tests.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Spec-in-repo plus automated validation is a concrete pattern for scaling quality; revives older engineering disciplines (TDD) now that the model removes their cost.
insights.quote: any time you have like a statement of what good looks like, get that into the repo and then Claude code review can make sure it's still matching what you set up to do.

insights.claim: Anthropic built an internal dashboard tracking swear words in user feedback as a frustration signal, started in September after the team observed rising frustration.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Illustrates that user-experience frustration, not just accuracy, is a tracked product metric for AI agents and a hard part of evals.
insights.quote: that was last September cuz we were all we were all seeing some frustrations... an engineer on the team of hey, we should maybe track swear words.

insights.claim: Anthropic's edge in spotting big opportunities comes from watching latent demand: it noticed non-coders using Claude Code, which motivated building Cowork to serve knowledge work beyond engineering.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Latent-demand observation is the stated mechanism behind Anthropic's expansion from coding into general knowledge work, signaling a deliberate TAM-expansion playbook.
insights.quote: co-work we noticed hey, a lot of folks that were not necessarily coders were were using Claude Code. Can we make that experience better?

insights.claim: Anthropic launched 'Claude for small business', bundling relevant plugins behind a toggle inside Cowork, after the team ran sessions with small businesses and found efficiency gains in invoicing and expensing.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A concrete new SMB product line and go-to-market segment for Anthropic, targeting invoicing/expensing pain points historically underserved by AI.
insights.quote: now we're launching Claude for small business. Which is really cool... inside co-work you just have this little toggle.

insights.claim: Fiona frames coding as possibly the biggest new business opportunity in history and knowledge work as the next target, with Anthropic deliberately going big on opportunities ahead of other labs.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Frames the strategic scale of the markets Anthropic is pursuing; useful for sizing the addressable opportunity behind its product roadmap.
insights.quote: coding. So far ahead. Like realizing this is a huge market... that's maybe the biggest new business opportunity in history.

insights.claim: Real users repeatedly use Cowork in unintended ways, such as a restaurant owner asking Claude to do competitive menu-price market analysis across comparable cuisines in her area.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Emergent, unplanned use cases are the leading indicator Anthropic mines for new features and markets; watch user behavior for product direction.
insights.quote: she goes, Hey Claude, look across my style of cuisine in this area is a comparable. And it came back with really cool almost like market analysis.

insights.claim: Fiona advises building something that is almost working at the edge of model capability, because when the next model lands it suddenly works and you are far ahead of competitors.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A capability-timing strategy: revisit previously failed automations after each model release, since exponential improvement converts near-misses into shipped features.
insights.quote: there might have been something I tried to automate that Claude wasn't quite good enough. And then actually in the next model, oh well, now it is good enough.

themes.theme: Team culture, adaptation, and what's lost

insights.claim: Fiona's team pairs 'high agency' (everyone proposes solutions and has freedom to build) with 'high accountability' anchored to an explicit hypothesis of what each person is trying to solve.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A management model for keeping autonomous, high-velocity AI teams aligned; agency without accountability is explicitly avoided.
insights.quote: high agency and then we say with high agency is also high accountability. So it's all about like making sure folks have that freedom to cook.

insights.claim: Fiona requires managers on her team to start as ICs and continue coding part-time as player-coaches, so they learn the codebase and build rapport before taking on people management.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A distinctive org-design choice for AI-native teams that keeps leaders close to the product and shortens manager onboarding.
insights.quote: if you actually start as IC first without a like worry of supporting people... give yourself that maker time to actually type deep into the code and learn the code base

insights.claim: Working primarily with agents created a loneliness problem on the Claude Code team, prompting them to start pairwise programming lunches and hackathons because watching how differently colleagues use the tools yields large learning gains.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A non-obvious second-order cost of agentic workflows; team-connection rituals become a deliberate retention and knowledge-sharing intervention.
insights.quote: it could start being a lonely experience cuz we all started just working with our agent so much. So recently we started a maybe a like a pairwise programming lunch.

insights.claim: The async, multi-agent workflow is increasing context-switching load, forcing Fiona to re-block dedicated focus time to catch up on the many agent jobs she kicked off, a problem she says she has not yet solved.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Context-switching overhead is an unsolved UX problem created by agent fleets; tooling that reduces it is an open product opportunity.
insights.quote: with routines and everything being more async, I I I think there is starting to be a high load on our context switching... I do see the context switching load increasing.

insights.claim: Fiona worries about training the next generation of engineers when traditional learning paths are obsolete, suggesting software engineering may move toward fellowship or apprenticeship models to teach the foundational 'double-click' understanding of dependencies.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Flags a structural risk in the engineering talent pipeline; how juniors gain depth without writing years of code is an open, unsolved question for the industry.
insights.quote: I wonder if it's um for software engineering, it's almost like you go more towards a fellowship or apprenticeship program.

insights.claim: Fiona identifies a growth mindset and the willingness to do something scary as the common trait among engineers thriving with AI, while frustration and resistance often stem from fear and a feeling of lost control.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: Adaptation, not raw skill, separates thriving from struggling engineers in the AI transition; a lens for assessing team and individual durability.
insights.quote: the growth mindset is is really, really has has really served me well... in terms of like the frustration, I think sometimes I also see a little bit of fear.

insights.claim: Fiona prioritizes dogfooding and anecdotal user evidence over dashboards, citing Jeff Bezos's view that when data and an anecdote conflict you should trust the anecdote, and noting metrics can mislead (e.g., counting sellers missed power sellers at Facebook Marketplace).
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A product-judgment heuristic relevant to evaluating AI products where UX quality resists quantification; over-indexing on metrics can hide real problems.
insights.quote: I think it was Jeff Bezos that said if you have the data and you have an anecdote, trust the anecdote over the data.

insights.claim: Fiona replaced six-month roadmaps with 'JIT' (just-in-time) monthly planning on a lightweight shared spreadsheet with weekly check-ins, after finding a six-month plan went stale within three months as the landscape changed.
insights.speaker: Fiona Fung
insights.so_what: A planning cadence calibrated to AI's rate of change; long-horizon roadmaps are explicitly judged obsolete for fast-moving AI product teams.
insights.quote: I I call it JIT planning now, like just-in-time planning... 6 months was too long. So, now... we try to do like a a month of planning, like really lightweight.
